hero.title: For the backend developer
hero.subtitle: This section turns backend/SolSignalModel1D_Backtest into a starting map: which projects shape the solution, where execution begins, how temporal boundaries work, which reports are published, and which layers catch time, data, and contract defects.

terms.description:
A day-key is the stable key of one trading day.

What it shows:
Daily windows, part of the split logic, and historical records are attached to it.

How to read it:
If the same timestamp becomes a different day-key in neighboring layers, the causal path, training split, and reports start diverging immediately.

NY windowing is the rule that slices time relative to the New York trading day.

What it shows:
It decides where the daily window starts and ends, how the day-key is computed, and where the boundary between already-known and future history sits.

How to read it:
If the rule breaks, the same bar can fall into different days and different train/test windows in adjacent layers.

BackgroundService is a background loop inside a .NET host that runs alongside normal HTTP endpoints.

What it shows:
It is useful for tasks that must keep the system state in sync without waiting for a manual user request.

How to read it:
In this project BackgroundService is used for the real forecast journal and for synchronizing [[developer-home-backfilled-history|backfilled history]] for current prediction.

Fail-fast is the rule that a broken input, a missing snapshot, or an invalid contract must not be hidden behind a quiet fallback.

What it shows:
The project prefers to stop with an explicit error at the source of the defect instead of dragging that defect deeper into the pipeline.

How to read it:
When the backend throws early, it is usually protecting an invariant rather than being unnecessarily strict.

A feature vector is the numeric set of features passed into the model during training or inference.

What it shows:
Every position in that vector must depend only on data that was allowed to exist at that moment.

How to read it:
Leakage tests often mutate the future and verify that the feature vector stays unchanged.

Coverage means data completeness for the window under analysis.

What it shows:
It answers whether the required days, candles, and trading-window slices are actually present.

How to read it:
If coverage is incomplete, [[developer-home-daily-rows|daily rows]], split boundaries, and downstream reports are already untrustworthy.

Example:
If part of the 1m window or a full working day is missing, that day cannot be treated as fully covered.

Backfilled history is the archive of past current-prediction reports that the backend rebuilds for already finished trading days.

What it shows:
It is not the live journal of the current day. It stores post-fact documents for past dates that can be opened by date or read as the latest ready archival report.

How to read it:
This layer is maintained by `CurrentPredictionHistorySyncWorker` and `ReportStorage`. The storage and API mechanics are detailed on the reports and API page.

Daily rows are day-level dataset records where one trading day is already collapsed into a single row.

What it shows:
A row contains time keys, [[developer-home-features|features]], day labels, and service fields used by training and inference.

How to read it:
This is not raw candle data. It is the prepared day-level layer built from 1m, 1h, and 6h history.

Baseline-exit is the canonical moment when the day window is treated as finished.

What it shows:
The train-versus-evaluation cut is anchored to the actual end of the trading window rather than to the trade entry time.

How to read it:
If a day enters training by its entry time but its baseline-exit already lands after the split boundary, that day must leave training.

purpose.question: What this backend does
purpose.answer: The backend builds a daily forecast for [[developer-home-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]], runs the same logic on history, and publishes ready [[developer-home-report-artifact|report artifacts]] that the API project `SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Api` serves to the frontend.

frameworks.question: What it targets
frameworks.answer: The main and test projects target net10.0; the analyzer projects stay on netstandard2.0 so that static checks can be plugged in separately from the execution runtime.

universe.question: Which data and instruments are already visible in execution
universe.answer: The entry point explicitly uses [[developer-home-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] as the working pair plus BTCUSDT and PAXGUSDT as context instruments with 1m, 1h, and 6h timeframes. Daily [[developer-home-features|features]] are then aligned to [[developer-home-day-key|day-key]] and [[developer-home-ny-windowing|NY windowing]].

entrypoints.question: Where execution starts
entrypoints.answer: The main entry is Program.cs. From there the execution contour is split between AppOrchestration/**, Prediction.cs, the backtest branch, Reports.Runner, and Api.

artifacts.question: What the backend persists and returns
artifacts.answer: File-based report storage already contains live current prediction, [[developer-home-backfilled-history|backfilled history]] current prediction, backtest summary, diagnostics, policy_branch_mega, PFI, and model stats. The API project reads those files and returns them to the frontend.

whatIsNotHere.question: What should not be expected here
whatIsNotHere.answer: HTTP endpoints mostly read already persisted reports. Heavy model and backtest recomputation does not run inside every request; there is no dedicated SQL or ORM project in the main solution, and report storage is file-based.

bullets.mainMap: The main structure map is SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.sln. SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Api.sln exists next to it, but that is only a narrow HTTP-focused solution rather than a separate architecture.
bullets.zones: At a high level the backend splits into the time and contract foundation, the [[developer-home-causal|causal]] decision layer, the [[developer-home-omniscient|omniscient]] post-fact evaluation layer, the reports and API delivery layer, and separate test and guard projects.
bullets.details: The detailed table of every project, its first-level folders, and its root files lives on the backend-structure page.

steps.loadData: The project first loads and refreshes Binance candles, indicators, and supporting series for the working instrument set.
steps.validateData: Then it runs [[developer-home-coverage|coverage]] and data-integrity checks: gaps, candle order, allowed windows, and the base time invariants.
steps.prepareRows: After that it builds causal [[developer-home-daily-rows|daily rows]] and [[developer-home-features|features]] without future access; this is where [[developer-home-day-key|day-key]] and the boundary against [[developer-home-leakage|leakage]] matter most.
steps.splitHistory: History is split by [[developer-home-baseline-exit|baseline-exit]] day-key rather than by the day of trade entry so that train and evaluation stay aligned to the actual close of the trading window.
steps.trainAndInfer: Training and inference come next: the offline train contour builds artifacts, while the working forecast uses them without mixing in [[developer-home-omniscient|omniscient]] knowledge of the future.
steps.buildReports: At the end the backend builds reports, prediction history, diagnostics, and API-ready documents that the API project serves next.

causalBoundary.title: How causal and omniscient stay separated

bullets.causalAssembly: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Causal is the decision layer. Its csproj references only Core and Core.Time, and analyzer `CAUSAL005` forbids references to Core.Omniscient, Core.Truth.Contracts, Core.Report.Contracts, and Core.Causal.ReadModel.
bullets.omniscientAssembly: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Omniscient is the post-fact evaluation layer. Its csproj already depends on Core.Causal, Core.Causal.ReadModel, Core.Truth.Contracts, Core.Infer, and Core.Train.Domain because it assembles day outcome, PnL, analytics, and snapshot artifacts on top of causal data.
bullets.oneWayBoundary: The dependency is one-way: [[developer-home-omniscient|omniscient]] may read [[developer-home-causal|causal]], but causal must not read omniscient. Otherwise day outcome and other post-fact fields would leak back into the decision layer.
bullets.howCausalityWorks: Causality is enforced by a combination of [[developer-home-ny-windowing|NY windowing]], [[developer-home-day-key|day-key]], building [[developer-home-daily-rows|daily rows]] and [[developer-home-features|features]] without future data, cutting history by [[developer-home-baseline-exit|baseline-exit]], and leakage plus analyzer guards.

delivery.title: How the backend delivers results outward

bullets.documents: File-based report storage contains ready documents: live current prediction, [[developer-home-backfilled-history|backfilled history]] for past dates, backtest summary, diagnostics, policy_branch_mega, PFI, and model stats. The API project reads those files and returns them to the frontend.
bullets.workers: Api contains two background [[developer-home-background-service|BackgroundService]] loops: one maintains the real forecast journal, and the other keeps [[developer-home-backfilled-history|backfilled history]] for current prediction in sync for past trading days.
bullets.failFast: The delivery contour relies on [[developer-home-fail-fast|fail-fast]]: a broken report, a missing report file, or an invalid contract must stop the request immediately.

tests.title: How the test contour is built

bullets.coverageShape: The current snapshot has about 578 tests in total: roughly 526 functional xUnit tests, 26 architecture tests, 20 analyzer tests, and 6 cache-path analyzer tests.
bullets.featureIsolation: [[developer-home-leakage|Leakage]] and model tests perform one hard check: they freeze all data before the entry boundary, then intentionally mutate only future candles after that boundary and recompute the [[developer-home-feature-vector|feature vector]], [[developer-home-daily-rows|daily rows]], and [[developer-home-features|features]] before it. Those values must stay identical because at entry time the backend had no right to know what happens later. Example 1: if changing the next day's candles alters even one volatility feature for the entry day, a future tail has leaked into the calculation. Example 2: if high/low values are changed after the entry window and yesterday's daily row changes its day label or prepared feature set, the causal boundary is broken. Light analogy: a Monday accounting report must not be rewritten just because someone edited Tuesday postings; if it changes, the future has already leaked backward.
bullets.datasetBoundary: Split and time tests build synthetic history on both sides of [[developer-home-baseline-exit|baseline-exit]] and verify that a day does not stay in training just because entry happened earlier while the actual window close already belongs to evaluation.
bullets.reportGuards: Report and storage tests intentionally create broken JSON, invalid provenance metadata, missing files, and forbidden legacy artifacts, then expect an explicit failure instead of silent repair.
bullets.selfCheck: Self-check tests build synthetic backtest records and verify that suspiciously perfect train accuracy or an impossible result shape is blocked before a normal run continues.

mainSuite.scope: Time, Binance candles, day-key, baseline-exit, leakage, ML, current prediction, backtest, report storage, API, and self-check.

archSuite.scope: Forbidden references between projects and layers, the causal versus omniscient boundary, and other architecture barriers.

analyzersSuite.scope: Roslyn rules that catch dangerous patterns during build.

cachePathSuite.scope: Cache-path conventions and tests for the analyzer rules themselves.

header.title: Backend map by solution and source folders
header.subtitle: This page fixes the main solution graph of backend/SolSignalModel1D_Backtest, the real source folders, and the root compile units of the current snapshot. It shows where the executable pipeline starts, where the base time and contract layers live, where the causal, train, infer, and omniscient contour runs, where reports and API are built, and which directories are not working source code.

coverage.text: This page combines the main SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.sln, the linked root-source folders, and the root-source files from the current backend/SolSignalModel1D_Backtest snapshot. The working tree includes only active source nodes with domain logic, orchestration, reports, API, and guards; generated, empty, and service directories are split out separately.

nodeKinds.text: The page uses four node kinds: solution project, root source folder, root source file, and excluded generated or service directory. If a node looks like a project by name but is not part of the main solution or has no active source code, it is marked as an exclusion rather than a working layer.

readingOrder.text: The normal reading order is: the root console project and compile-linked AppOrchestration source, then Core.Time and Core, then causal, read-model, train, and infer, then omniscient and report contracts, then reports and api, and finally tests, arch tests, and analyzers. That order matches the real ProjectReference graph and the actual data flow through the pipeline.

nonGoals.text: This page does not replace reading the code. Its job is different: narrow the search to the correct assembly, the correct folder, and the correct root file, and also make it explicit where not to look.

why-coverage.text: The repository layout and the compile graph do not match one-to-one here: part of the runtime code is brought in through linked compile from root folders, so a plain list of project directories is not enough.

why-nodeKinds.text: Without this distinction it is easy to mistake an empty snapshot folder for a working layer or, conversely, miss compile-linked source outside the project directory.

why-readingOrder.text: That order is not cosmetic: it follows where an upstream invariant is created first and in which downstream layer it only shows up as a symptom.

why-nonGoals.text: The main failure mode without such a map is opening the nearest folder by name and treating it as the cause location even though the real boundary sits in a different assembly.

terms.description:
A solution is the `.sln` file that lists projects and keeps the full build composition together.

What it shows:
It is not the same thing as the folder tree: neighboring directories may exist without belonging to the solution.

How to read it:
On this page the solution is the primary map of working [[backend-structure-assembly|assemblies]].

An assembly is one compiled `csproj` output.

What it shows:
Architecture boundaries are enforced between assemblies, not between similarly named folders.

How to read it:
If two pieces of code live in different assemblies, their dependency direction matters even when they sit next to each other on disk.

A day-key is the stable key of a trading day.

What it shows:
Time windows, [[backend-structure-truth-labels|truth labels]], and part of the [[backend-structure-feature-stamps|feature stamps]] logic are attached to it.

How to read it:
If two layers compute a different day-key for the same timestamp, the rest of the daily logic starts diverging immediately.

NY windowing is the rule that slices time relative to the New York trading day.

What it shows:
It affects the [[backend-structure-day-key|day-key]], valid input boundaries, and checks against [[backend-structure-leakage|leakage]].

How to read it:
When this rule breaks, the same timestamp lands in different windows in different layers.

Temporal tokens are service markers that carry the boundary of what was already known at a given moment.

What it shows:
They keep [[backend-structure-causal|causal]], training, and reports on the same time contract.

How to read it:
They are often the first place to inspect when a temporal boundary starts drifting.

Feature stamps are time markers and lineage hints attached to features.

What it shows:
They show when a feature belongs and whether it can be used without [[backend-structure-leakage|leakage]].

How to read it:
A wrong stamp can make a future feature look valid even though it arrived too late.

Truth labels are the factual post-fact labels of a day or trade outcome.

What it shows:
They appear after the window closes and therefore must not enter the [[backend-structure-causal|causal]] input.

How to read it:
If they become available too early, the historical check stops being honest.

DTO means Data Transfer Object.

What it shows:
It carries data between layers and defines the shape of the payload.

How to read it:
A DTO should not hide domain decisions inside itself; otherwise layer boundaries become unreadable.

A read-model is a projection prepared for one consumer layer.

What it shows:
It simplifies access to data but does not create a new domain truth.

How to read it:
If the read-model starts owning its own business rules, the project gets duplicated logic in two places.

Infer is the runtime branch that applies an already trained model to the current feature set.

What it shows:
It consumes training artifacts, but it does not train the model itself.

How to read it:
If the issue exists only in the live prediction while the training artifacts are already correct, the search often narrows to infer.

The train boundary separates data that is allowed into training from data that still belongs to the future.

What it shows:
A defect here turns into [[backend-structure-leakage|leakage]] very quickly.

How to read it:
The usual symptom is overly good train quality followed by a hard drop on new data.

ProjectReference is an explicit `csproj` reference from one project to another.

What it shows:
The `ProjectReference` graph shows which [[backend-structure-assembly|assembly]] is allowed to know about which other one at compile time.

How to read it:
That graph matters more than simple folder proximity.

Compile-linked source means files that physically live outside the project folder but are compiled into its [[backend-structure-assembly|assembly]] through `Compile Include`.

What it shows:
Because of that, the directory layout and the actual project code do not match one-to-one here.

How to read it:
If this is ignored, a project can look almost empty even though important code lives at the repository root.

Orchestration is the launch-routing layer.

What it shows:
It decides who builds data, who runs reports, and how internal computation is connected to external delivery.

How to read it:
It is glue between model core, storage, and delivery, not the place where domain truth is created.

Bootstrap is the startup stage where environment, configuration, and initial dependencies are assembled before the main pipeline begins.

What it shows:
If bootstrap is broken, the process usually fails before the first business computation.

How to read it:
This is process startup, not the domain flow itself.

An endpoint is one HTTP entry point in the API.

What it shows:
It owns the route and request parameters, but it should not absorb report-building or model logic.

How to read it:
Once an endpoint starts recalculating upstream data, the boundary between delivery and computation becomes blurry.

A guard is a protective rule that should stop an error before it travels further down the pipeline.

What it shows:
A guard can be runtime, test-level, or compile-time.

How to read it:
A good guard catches the cause before the defect turns into a noisy downstream symptom.

Preflight is a fast preliminary check before a heavy run.

What it shows:
It does not replace the full test suite; it cuts off obviously broken state early.

How to read it:
On this page it is closely related to quick sanity checks and operational guards.

An arch test is a test for an architecture boundary.

What it shows:
It checks dependency direction and forbidden references between [[backend-structure-assembly|assemblies]], not business output.

How to read it:
It is useful where a normal runtime scenario would catch the problem too late.

An analyzer is a static compile-time code check.

What it shows:
It catches forbidden patterns during build and complements an [[backend-structure-arch-test|arch test]], which still runs as a regular test.

How to read it:
This is the earliest protection layer: the defect becomes visible before app startup and even before test execution.

overview.text: This block first reduces the main SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.sln to one table, then splits it into the Foundation, Model pipeline, Runtime / delivery, and Quality zones, and only after that moves into the root files and dependency rules. The order is deliberate: the full list of assemblies should be visible before individual projects and files are read in detail.

why-overview.text: The table has to come first because the solution exposes the full compile graph immediately, while the tree below only expands selected nodes in detail.

mainSolution.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.sln is the canonical source of structure. It shows the main compile graph: which projects actually belong to the backend and in what composition they are built together.

apiSolution.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Api.sln is an auxiliary solution for local work around the HTTP layer. It is useful for endpoint changes, but it must not be used as the map of causal, train, reports, or the full startup path.

why-mainSolution.text: The main solution fixes the active project set rather than a local slice created for one delivery task.

why-apiSolution.text: The API solution hides the upstream layers, so it is good for local HTTP debugging but not for reading the whole architecture.

foundationZone.title: Foundation: time, base types, and contracts

foundationProjects.text: The Foundation zone contains Core.Time, Core, Core.Config, Core.Report.Contracts, and Core.Truth.Contracts. This is where the temporal canon, base types, coverage rules, truth contracts, and the shape of persisted report documents live.

foundationUsage.text: If a day key, time boundary, coverage rule, truth label, or the base report DTO changes, the cause should be searched in Foundation first. Edits in train, reports, or api usually only expose drift that started here.

why-foundationProjects.text: These projects sit low in the graph because causal, train, reports, and delivery layers all depend on them at the same time.

why-foundationUsage.text: When a shared time or contract invariant breaks, downstream layers only show the secondary symptom rather than the source.

modelPipelineZone.title: Model pipeline: causal, infer, train, omniscient

pipelineProjects.text: The model pipeline starts with Core.Causal and Core.Causal.ReadModel, then branches into Core.Infer, Core.Train, Core.Train.Domain, and Core.Omniscient. This is the layer of datasets and models, not storage, HTTP, or report delivery.

pipelineUsage.text: Core.Causal holds decision-time data without future knowledge. Core.Infer owns runtime prediction, Core.Train and Core.Train.Domain own offline training and the train boundary, and Core.Omniscient owns post-fact evaluation after the day closes.

why-pipelineProjects.text: These projects are separated by what they are allowed to know about the future: causal works without it, while omniscient may see the actual outcome only after the day closes.

why-pipelineUsage.text: If those boundaries are mixed up, the model layer starts leaking future knowledge or blending runtime and offline scenarios.

runtimeProjects.text: The Runtime / delivery zone contains Infra, AppOrchestration, AppOrchestration.Interop, Reports, Reports.Runner, and Api. This is where domain results are connected to files, the startup route, the report archive, and HTTP endpoints.

runtimeUsage.text: If core data is already correct but startup, orchestration, report-document build, archive rebuild, or endpoint delivery breaks, the primary search usually starts in this zone.

why-runtimeProjects.text: IO, orchestration, and transport concerns stay separate so that Foundation and the model pipeline do not depend on the filesystem, the host, or the HTTP contract.

why-runtimeUsage.text: When the data is correct but result delivery breaks, the cause usually sits in orchestration, storage, or report/API wiring rather than the model core.

qualityProjects.text: The Quality zone contains Tests, SanityChecks, ArchTests, Analyzers, Analyzers.Tests, CachePath.Analyzers, and CachePath.Analyzers.Tests. It does not build runtime output; it catches behavioral, architectural, and static violations.

qualityUsage.text: When a new guard is needed, choose the signal type first: a behavioral test for a scenario, an arch test for dependency direction, an analyzer for a compile-time ban, or a sanity check for a fast preflight signal.

why-qualityProjects.text: These projects are split because each guard type catches a violation at a different stage: during execution, in dependency shape, or already at compile time.

why-qualityUsage.text: The wrong guard gives either a noisy test or a signal that arrives too late to hold the boundary in place.

consoleProject.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest is the root executable assembly. It contains Main, the partial Program, and the top route that starts the full pipeline run.

consoleVsOrchestration.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.AppOrchestration is not a separate host but the orchestration library. The root executable calls it through interop and linked source, so the two nodes must be read together but not confused.

why-consoleProject.text: If the executable shell is not separated from the orchestration library, the startup route quickly turns into an unreadable mix of layers.

why-consoleVsOrchestration.text: This boundary shows where the process starts and where the library orchestration branch begins.

rootFiles.title: Which root files to read first

rootFilesSummary.text: Program.cs defines the global startup order. HostMain.cs loads AppOrchestration. Prediction.cs, DailyRows.cs, Policies.cs, DelayedA.cs, and SlModel.cs expose separate branches of the root scenario: prediction records, daily rows, the policy catalog, DelayedA, and the SL contour.

rootFilesUsage.text: The safest reading order is Program.cs and HostMain.cs first, then DailyRows.cs and Prediction.cs, and only after that Policies.cs, DelayedA.cs, and SlModel.cs. Otherwise a local branch is easy to mistake for the global startup path.

why-rootFilesSummary.text: The root layer is split across several compile units because startup, dataset build, policies, and specialized branches would be unreadable inside one giant Program file.

why-rootFilesUsage.text: The reading order matters: without the global startup route, specialized root files look like entry points even though they execute later.

dependencyRules.title: How to use the dependency ladder

dependencyLadder.text: The main dependency ladder is: Core.Time and Core -> Core.Causal and Core.Causal.ReadModel -> Core.Infer, Core.Train, Core.Train.Domain, Core.Truth.Contracts -> Core.Omniscient and Core.Report.Contracts -> Infra, AppOrchestration, AppOrchestration.Interop -> Reports -> Api -> Tests, ArchTests, and Analyzers.

dependencyUsage.text: Every new reference should be checked against this ladder. A later layer must not pull domain logic backward, and Foundation must not know about report or HTTP delivery; a convenient shortcut across the boundary almost always damages the meaning of the layer.

why-dependencyLadder.text: The ladder matters because different backend zones are allowed to know different amounts about the future and operate at different stages of result delivery.

why-dependencyUsage.text: A wrong dependency direction first looks like a convenient refactor and later turns into leakage, transport coupling, or a blurred contract boundary.

what.text: The main solution brings core, orchestration, reports, api, tests, analyzers, and contract projects into one architectural slice. It is the source that shows which assemblies actually participate in the main backend contour.

whenOpen.text: Open this file first whenever the folder tree does not make it clear whether a node is a separate project, compile-linked source, or just a neighboring directory.

pitfalls.text: This solution does not guarantee that all code physically lives inside identically named project folders. Root partial files and AppOrchestration source stay outside the project directories while still being part of the compile graph.

why-what.text: This is where the real set of assemblies that form the main runtime and test graph becomes visible.

why-whenOpen.text: The folder layout alone barely exposes linked source, so the entry point for analysis must be chosen from the solution rather than the directory name.

why-pitfalls.text: If linked compile units are ignored, it is easy to lose the real startup path and attribute root behavior to the wrong assembly.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Api.sln is a narrow solution around the HTTP layer. It is useful for local startup and API debugging, but it does not describe the full backend graph.

whenOpen.text: Go here when the change is truly limited to the HTTP surface: endpoint wiring, DTOs, web host, swagger, or read-only delivery behavior.

pitfalls.text: This solution is not enough to reason about causal, train, infer, report build, or the root orchestration layers. Looking only at it makes the API look like the center of the system even though it is a downstream node.

why-what.text: A separate solution is needed for the narrow task of running and debugging the HTTP contour without the full solution graph.

why-whenOpen.text: When the problem is limited to the delivery surface, the full solution only adds noise and slows down localization.

why-pitfalls.text: If this solution is used as the architectural map of the whole backend, the upstream layers simply disappear from view.

what.text: The root SolSignalModel1D_Backtest project is the executable shell of the pipeline. It contains the executable host, the top-level console scenario, the root partial Program, and the compile units that pull bootstrap, dataset build, prediction records, diagnostics, and report rebuild into one run.

separation.text: The project is kept separate from SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.AppOrchestration so that the executable shell does not dissolve into the orchestration assembly. This split makes the difference between the process entry point and the orchestration library layer explicit.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the problem is visible in the full console run: startup, bootstrap, daily-row build, train/apply, prediction record generation, report invocation, or the top diagnostics branch.

dependencies.text: The project depends on Core, Core.Infer, Core.Train.Domain, Infra, and AppOrchestration.Interop, while SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.AppOrchestration is brought in as a separate project reference for the orchestration branch. This node makes it clear which layers are allowed inside the executable shell and which must arrive only through interop or contracts.

keyFiles.text: The key compile units here are Program.cs, HostMain.cs, Prediction.cs, DailyRows.cs, Policies.cs, DelayedA.cs, and SlModel.cs. Together they form the main root scenario even though they are physically split across several files and partial classes.

pitfalls.text: New contributors often look for orchestration only inside the SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.AppOrchestration project folder and miss the root partial files. That makes the real call order seem chaotic even though it is split between the root project, linked AppOrchestration source, and the interop layer.

why-what.text: This is the real executable entry surface, not a random historical project next to the solution.

why-separation.text: The separation keeps the startup shell, orchestration, and the omniscient branch from collapsing into one uncontrolled assembly.

why-whenOpen.text: If the failure is visible only in the full pipeline run, jumping straight to Reports or Api is usually too late because the cause is often formed earlier in the root flow.

why-dependencies.text: The project references here expose the allowed boundaries between the executable shell and the library layers.

why-keyFiles.text: These files are exactly what creates the illusion that the main logic is scattered across the root without structure even though they combine into one controlled scenario.

why-pitfalls.text: If the root partial files are skipped, the causal order of calls is read incorrectly and downstream layers start to look like the starting point.

what.text: Program.cs contains the Main method of the full backend pipeline: it picks the as-of point, starts data bootstrap, builds prediction records, activates diagnostics, the SL contour, and report calls.

whenOpen.text: Open this file when it is unclear in what order the backend parts run and which branch executes before another.

pitfalls.text: This file alone does not show the whole picture: a visible part of the logic is moved into neighboring root partial files and compile-linked AppOrchestration source.

why-what.text: This is where the top orchestration order is fixed.

why-whenOpen.text: When the startup order is unclear, localizing the cause inside deeper layers becomes random.

why-pitfalls.text: The partial Program is distributed across several compile units, so reading only Program.cs gives an incomplete execution map.

what.text: HostMain.cs implements a separate host that loads SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.AppOrchestration through reflection and calls its Main without tightly coupling the executable shell to the orchestration assembly.

whenOpen.text: Open this file when the application cannot find the AppOrchestration DLL, fails before the normal Main, or behaves differently depending on how it is launched.

pitfalls.text: Such early failures are easy to misread as lower-level infrastructure problems even though they happen earlier at assembly discovery and type lookup time.

why-what.text: The reflection bridge is needed so the orchestration assembly can remain separate from the root executable shell.

why-whenOpen.text: If the failure happens before the normal business logic begins, the assembly loading mechanism is the right place to inspect.

why-pitfalls.text: Early startup failures often masquerade as dependency problems even though the cause sits in the bridge between two assemblies.

what.text: Prediction.cs connects causal rows, minute candles, the train boundary token, and AppOrchestration.Interop into the route that builds prediction records.

whenOpen.text: Open this file when current prediction record generation breaks, record fields change, or the result diverges between the root run and downstream report build.

pitfalls.text: The file looks like the place where all prediction logic lives, but the real transformations are distributed across interop, infer, and train-domain contracts.

why-what.text: This node shows where the root shell translates upstream data into the prediction record contract.

why-whenOpen.text: If the record itself is broken rather than the model or the report, the build route is the right starting point.

why-pitfalls.text: Without the interop and train-domain layers this file looks self-contained even though it only connects lower-level contracts.

what.text: DailyRows.cs builds causal daily rows, runs truth labeling, leakage audit, and day-coverage checks. It is a key root file for understanding how 6h, 1m, and indicator data become a labeled dataset.

whenOpen.text: Open this file when daily rows, day keys, truth labels, or leakage signals start to diverge from expectations.

pitfalls.text: The name makes it look like the file is only about rows, but in practice it holds the boundary between the time canon, labeling, and dataset sanity.

why-what.text: This is where the point becomes visible at which upstream market data is first connected to downstream truth and coverage contracts.

why-whenOpen.text: If the issue belongs to the daily dataset, jumping too late into train or reports already hides the cause.

why-pitfalls.text: The file name does not reveal how many time and truth invariants pass through it.

what.text: Policies.cs builds the catalog of leverage and policy modes for backtest and runtime tables: fixed, risk-aware, dynamic, and spot-cap variants.

whenOpen.text: Open this file when branch names, cap rules, leverage mapping, or policy execution tables begin to diverge from the expected set of modes.

pitfalls.text: A change here rarely behaves like pure configuration: one edit affects backtest branches, report tables, and the interpretation of downstream metrics at the same time.

why-what.text: This is the node where the model signal is translated into a constrained set of policy modes later consumed by many layers.

why-whenOpen.text: If the composition of policy branches is broken, downstream tables merely reflect the primary error.

why-pitfalls.text: Because of its name, the file is often mistaken for a harmless constant list even though it holds an important domain boundary.

what.text: DelayedA.cs serves a separate DelayedA post-processing route over already built records with 1h and 1m candle context.

whenOpen.text: Open this file when the DelayedA scenario is the one breaking while the main baseline pipeline stays stable.

pitfalls.text: The wrapper is thin here: the numeric behavior is defined lower in the stack, so fixing only this file often treats a symptom.

why-what.text: DelayedA is isolated because it is not the main baseline flow but a dedicated post-processing branch over already built records.

why-whenOpen.text: If the baseline pipeline works while DelayedA does not, starting with a broad orchestration sweep is wasteful.

why-pitfalls.text: A wrapper file alone can make the logic look local even though the cause may live lower in interop and data coverage.

what.text: SlModel.cs trains and applies a dedicated offline SL model through interop runtime, the gap allowlist, and special debug routes.

whenOpen.text: Open this file when the stop-loss model, its debug metrics, or gap-sensitive behavior starts diverging from expectations.

pitfalls.text: The file does not explain the full SL contour by itself: key parts sit in the interop runtime, train records, and allowlist checks.

why-what.text: The SL contour is isolated because its lifecycle is not reducible to one flag in the main prediction flow.

why-whenOpen.text: If stop-loss decisions themselves diverge, the dedicated SL route is the right place to inspect rather than the general inference path.

why-pitfalls.text: A superficial fix in the wrapper file hides the fact that the numeric behavior is defined deeper in the stack.

foundationBoundary.text: Core.Time and Core are the foundation of the backend. Core.Time holds the temporal canon and anti-leakage semantics, while Core holds the base domain primitives on top of which the causal, train, and omniscient layers are built.

contractProjects.text: Core.Config, Core.Report.Contracts, and Core.Truth.Contracts are shared contract layers. They fix coverage rules, truth labeling, and the persisted report shape in one place so downstream layers do not invent their own versions.

timeAndCore.text: If a base time type, Optional contract, candle timeframe, or truth DTO breaks here, downstream layers usually show only the secondary symptom. That is why the foundation layers must be read before most application-level assemblies.

commonPitfall.text: The most common confusion in this zone is mixing domain data contracts with runtime implementation. Contract projects describe the shape and invariant rather than the builder, endpoint, or training flow.

why-foundationBoundary.text: These layers create the upstream invariants on which almost all other projects stand.

why-contractProjects.text: Shared contracts are needed so that the same concept does not live in five different assemblies with slightly different meanings.

why-timeAndCore.text: If the foundation is already broken, investigating downstream symptoms gives a false picture.

why-commonPitfall.text: The boundary between data shape and computation is critical here: once it is broken, the whole dependency ladder is quickly blurred.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core holds the base domain types and shared primitives needed by almost every other project. This is where Optional contracts, base candle and timeframe abstractions, gap diagnostics, and common diagnostic helpers live.

separation.text: Core is separated from the causal, train, and omniscient layers because it must not contain assumptions about a specific pipeline stage or about future data being available.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the issue touches base candle primitives, timeframe mapping, Optional wrappers, common exceptions, or shared diagnostic contracts.

dependencies.text: Core is depended on by Core.Causal, Core.Train, Core.Infer, Core.Omniscient, Infra, and part of the delivery layers. If a base type is broken here, the error spreads across several assemblies at once.

pitfalls.text: Do not move causal split logic, model-specific behavior, or report DTOs here just because they are used in several places. Core must remain the layer of shared invariants rather than a warehouse for everything reusable.

why-what.text: This project is needed as the shared foundation; without it downstream layers begin duplicating base types and diverging semantically.

why-separation.text: As soon as Core contains logic for a specific pipeline stage, the layer stops being neutral and starts pulling forbidden dependencies.

why-whenOpen.text: If the defect is already visible at the level of a base type or shared primitive, moving into deeper assemblies only masks the source.

why-dependencies.text: The wide fan-out of this project makes it one of the most sensitive points for base-contract errors.

why-pitfalls.text: Trying to dump everything shared here breaks the architecture faster than local duplication inside a specific layer.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Time defines the temporal canon of the backend: [[backend-structure-day-key|day keys]], [[backend-structure-ny-windowing|NY windowing]], [[backend-structure-temporal-tokens|temporal tokens]], [[backend-structure-feature-stamps|feature stamps]], and protection from [[backend-structure-leakage|time leakage]]. It is the main layer that defines what counts as correct time for the rest of the [[backend-structure-assembly|assemblies]].

separation.text: The project is separated from Core and from the [[backend-structure-causal|causal]] layers because temporal semantics must be uniform across the backend and cannot depend on a specific dataset builder or report flow.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when [[backend-structure-day-key|day keys]] diverge, the [[backend-structure-ny-windowing|NY window]] breaks, [[backend-structure-leakage|time leakage]] appears, or different layers start interpreting the same timestamp differently.

dependencies.text: Core.Time is depended on by Causal, Train.Domain, Truth.Contracts, Omniscient, and the test guards. A defect here almost never stays local and quickly surfaces as a cascade of strange dates and split boundaries.

pitfalls.text: New contributors often look for the temporal cause inside Core.Causal or the train split, although the canonical time types and the key restrictions live here.

why-what.text: Without a single temporal canon, each downstream layer starts silently inventing its own day-key and window rules.

why-separation.text: Temporal semantics are a cross-cutting invariant, so they cannot be hidden inside one domain project.

why-whenOpen.text: If strange dates, windows, or boundary tokens are already visible, moving to later layers only takes you farther from the cause.

why-dependencies.text: High temporal coupling makes this project a critical trust point for the whole pipeline.

why-pitfalls.text: When the time cause is searched inside causal rows, the root problem has already changed clothes into a downstream symptom.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Config holds small but important cross-project domain configuration blocks. In the current snapshot the key node here is DayCoverage, which contains the policy, reason catalog, and report for day-coverage assessment.

separation.text: The project is separated because day-coverage rules are needed by several layers at once but should live neither in infra, nor in reports, nor in a specific model assembly.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the day-coverage classification rules, the coverage reason codes, or the document used to pass coverage downstream are changing.

dependencies.text: This project is used near the root dataset build and truth-related flows; its data affects how gaps and incomplete trading days are interpreted.

pitfalls.text: Do not confuse Core.Config with infrastructure application configuration. It stores neither appsettings nor DI setup but domain rules that become part of the computation itself.

why-what.text: Rules like these belong in a separate project because they are consumed as a domain contract rather than as startup plumbing.

why-separation.text: If coverage rules live inside one downstream layer, other layers start interpreting incomplete days in their own way.

why-whenOpen.text: When the reason for an incomplete day or the coverage classification diverges, jumping into train or reports is already too late.

why-dependencies.text: These rules affect which days are considered valid for downstream logic, so their impact is wider than the project size suggests.

why-pitfalls.text: The name Config easily suggests technical settings even though this project contains domain logic about day quality and completeness.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Report.Contracts holds the DTOs and document contracts for backtest, current prediction, and model-level reports. It is the layer through which Reports and Api agree on a stable data shape.

separation.text: The contracts are separated from Reports and Api so that the shape of a persisted document does not depend on builder implementation or HTTP wiring.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the report DTO set, document block names, the model snapshot, or a backtest contract that must be read identically by the builder, storage, and endpoint changes.

dependencies.text: These contracts are used by Reports, Reports.Runner, Api, and part of the test layers. Any incompatible edit here immediately hits both build and delivery surfaces at the same time.

pitfalls.text: Do not move builder logic, storage rules, or HTTP-specific details here. A contract project must describe the data shape rather than how it is calculated or delivered.

why-what.text: A stable report contract is needed as the common meeting point for the builder, storage, and api.

why-separation.text: If the contracts merge with Reports or Api, the builder and the delivery layer start changing the document shape independently.

why-whenOpen.text: When the document structure itself diverges, searching the end-to-end flow without looking at the contract layer is pointless.

why-dependencies.text: The wide fan-out makes this project a high-compatibility point: an error here breaks several downstream paths at once.

why-pitfalls.text: The contract layer must stay thin; once runtime logic is dragged into it, the boundary between shape and computation disappears.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Truth.Contracts describes truth-related data and rules: labeled rows, forward outcomes, baseline windows, guards for future-looking windows, and shared labeling components.

separation.text: The project is separated because truth and labeling are needed by the root dataset build, omniscient evaluation, and the test guards at once, but must not dissolve into either the causal layer or reports.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when truth labels, forward outcome windows, baseline guards, or shared labeling rules diverge between the root build and the omniscient contour.

dependencies.text: This layer is used by DailyRows.cs, Core.Train.Domain, Core.Omniscient, and part of the leakage and time guards. It sits between causal data and post-fact truth semantics.

pitfalls.text: A common confusion is that causal rows and truth contracts sound similar but belong to different levels. Causal describes what is available at decision time, while truth contracts define how the actual outcome is labeled afterwards.

why-what.text: Truth and labeling are better kept as a shared layer; otherwise one downstream project starts dictating the shape of the actual outcome to everyone else.

why-separation.text: This keeps a clear boundary between data available at decision time and data that becomes known only later.

why-whenOpen.text: If the issue belongs to a label or a truth window, trying to start with reports or api only hides the primary error.

why-dependencies.text: This layer ties together several pipeline branches, so an error in a truth contract quickly becomes systemic.

why-pitfalls.text: The most dangerous confusion here is moving future-looking logic back into the causal layer and breaking the causal boundary.

pipelineBoundary.text: The model pipeline is split across several assemblies: Causal is responsible for honest decision-time data, Train for offline training, Infer for runtime application, Train.Domain for shared boundary semantics, and Omniscient for post-fact evaluation.

causalVsOmniscient.text: The main boundary here is causal versus omniscient. Causal does not know the future and is valid for taking decisions; Omniscient knows the actual outcome and is valid for evaluation, reports, and backtest analytics.

trainInferFlow.text: Train builds offline artifacts and train/backtest datasets. Infer uses the already built artifacts at runtime. Train.Domain connects them through a shared understanding of the train boundary, split, and current-prediction scope.

whereToStart.text: If a data row, feature, split, or day key is broken, start with Causal, Core.Time, and Train.Domain. If the upstream inputs are already correct but the prediction or score diverges, narrowing the search to Infer and the train artifacts is much safer.

why-pipelineBoundary.text: Splitting the pipeline by assembly preserves different lifecycles and different trust boundaries.

why-causalVsOmniscient.text: Without this boundary the project quickly loses the ability to prove that a runtime decision was made without knowledge of the actual outcome.

why-trainInferFlow.text: Offline and runtime contours must understand splits and artifacts in the same way, but they do not need to live in one implementation.

why-whereToStart.text: The right starting point saves the most time exactly in the model pipeline, where downstream symptoms are numerous.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Causal builds causal rows, features, and trading-aware data without future knowledge. It is the main project for the decision-time representation of the market.

separation.text: The project is separated from the omniscient layer because it cannot use post-fact truth, forward outcomes, or metrics that become known only after the day ends.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the problem touches causal-row values, feature stamps, split-safe market data, trading constraints, or feature preparation before inference.

dependencies.text: Core.Causal depends on Core and Core.Time, and upstream from it sit ReadModel, Train, Infer, Omniscient, and part of the test guards. It is the central upstream layer of the model pipeline.

pitfalls.text: A common mistake is moving truth logic, final backtest metrics, or report DTOs into this project. Once the causal layer starts knowing the future, its main value is lost.

why-what.text: This is the main layer that keeps the data in the form of “what was known at decision time.”

why-separation.text: Without this boundary, the causal honesty of the model becomes a declaration without technical support.

why-whenOpen.text: If the defect already appears in causal rows or feature build, looking inside infer or reports is too late.

why-dependencies.text: The high fan-out makes this project one of the main causal points of the whole backend.

why-pitfalls.text: The most dangerous degradation path is silently adding future-looking semantics into the causal dataset.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Causal.ReadModel holds read-oriented views of causal data and a thin infrastructure layer around them.

separation.text: The project is separated from the heavy causal build so that read-model shape and data access do not dissolve into the main feature logic.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the causal data is already built but its read-oriented view or the technical reading path is broken.

dependencies.text: The project sits next to Core.Causal and is used where a lighter way of reading the causal representation is needed without rebuilding the full upstream graph.

pitfalls.text: Do not confuse the read model with the source of causal truth. It describes a convenient reading shape rather than the primary data-building logic.

why-what.text: A read model is useful as a separate layer when the same causal entity is needed in a lighter reading shape.

why-separation.text: This keeps data shape and infrastructure reading separate from the main causal build.

why-whenOpen.text: If the problem is no longer in the build itself but in how the data is read downstream, this is the right place to inspect.

why-dependencies.text: The layer is small, but it separates upstream calculation from downstream consumption.

why-pitfalls.text: New contributors often jump back into Core.Causal even though the error already lives at the boundary between read shape and infra.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Infer applies trained artifacts to runtime data and assembles current predictions.

separation.text: Infer is separated from Train because applying the model at runtime and training it require different contracts, different failure modes, and different levels of data access.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the input causal rows are already correct but current prediction or day-level inference starts diverging from the expected result.

dependencies.text: The project depends on causal and train-domain contracts, while the root prediction flow, reports, and api rely on its output downstream.

pitfalls.text: Do not look here for dataset-build or train-split errors. Infer assumes the upstream causal and boundary contracts are already defined.

why-what.text: Runtime inference is isolated because it works on already-built model artifacts and current data.

why-separation.text: This prevents train logic from leaking into the runtime layer and mixing offline and online contours.

why-whenOpen.text: If the upstream data is already verified, the cause is narrowed down to model application and score assembly.

why-dependencies.text: Infer is the bridge between model artifacts and the delivery surface, so it is sensitive to both contracts at once.

why-pitfalls.text: Trying to fix dataset build problems here usually treats only the manifestation, not the cause.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Train holds the offline training pipeline: train and backtest dataset preparation, the ML contour, and trading-aware train computations.

separation.text: Train is separated from Infer so that offline training, backtest preparation, and runtime application do not share the same assembly boundary.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the problem appears in train datasets, offline feature preparation, the backtest-oriented train loop, or model artifact build.

dependencies.text: Train depends on Core.Causal, Core.Time, and Train.Domain and then feeds Infer, Omniscient, and report builds through its artifacts and results.

pitfalls.text: Do not mix HTTP, report documents, or delivery routes into Train. It must remain an offline computation and artifact-preparation layer.

why-what.text: Offline training needs its own layer because it has a different lifecycle and input set than runtime inference.

why-separation.text: This prevents model training and model application from collapsing into one contour with blurred invariants.

why-whenOpen.text: If the defect lives in the train artifacts, checking downstream prediction only wastes time.

why-dependencies.text: Train is upstream for model artifacts, so an error here can look like a systemic failure in several lower layers.

why-pitfalls.text: When the offline layer starts knowing about delivery, the architectural boundary between computation and delivery disappears.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Train.Domain stores train and test split contracts, the training scope, and boundary-derivation rules. It is the domain layer that defines how the training window is separated from runtime current prediction.

separation.text: The project is separated because split boundary and train-domain rules are needed by the root flow, infer, and train at once but must not sink inside one implementation.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the train boundary token, train/test split derivation, current prediction training scope, or a shared dataset contract is broken.

dependencies.text: Train.Domain is used by the root partial files, Core.Train, and Core.Infer. It is the layer that keeps a shared meaning for the boundary between offline and runtime parts of the pipeline.

pitfalls.text: A common mistake is to hide a split rule inside Train or Infer. Then the two sides of the pipeline stop agreeing on where the training window ends.

why-what.text: The train-domain boundary must live as a shared contract rather than as a private detail of one implementation.

why-separation.text: This lets several layers read the same train boundary in the same way.

why-whenOpen.text: If the boundary between train and runtime is what breaks, looking only in the pure ML layer is insufficient.

why-dependencies.text: This layer is thin, but it defines one of the most important invariants in the whole pipeline.

why-pitfalls.text: Different local split rules in different assemblies almost guarantee systemic desynchronization.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Omniscient works with post-fact data: backtest, actual outcomes, omniscient analytics, and aggregates that can be calculated only after the day ends.

separation.text: The project is separated from Causal because knowledge of the actual outcome is allowed here. It is a different trust contour: useful for evaluation and reporting but forbidden as the source of a runtime decision.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the problem appears in backtest calculations, omniscient analytics, post-fact data fusion, or final evaluation tables.

dependencies.text: Omniscient depends on causal, truth contracts, and train artifacts, and then feeds reports and part of diagnostics downstream. It is downstream relative to the causal contour but upstream for final evaluation.

pitfalls.text: The most dangerous confusion is accidentally pulling omniscient semantics back into causal or runtime inference. Then the evaluation layer begins influencing the decision layer.

why-what.text: This project is needed so post-fact analysis has its own legitimate place and does not contaminate decision-time data.

why-separation.text: Without a hard separation, the evaluation layer starts silently telling runtime things it should not know.

why-whenOpen.text: If the error is already visible in backtest or post-fact metrics, the causal layer may be fully correct.

why-dependencies.text: Omniscient sits exactly where upstream causal truth turns into final evaluation and report semantics.

why-pitfalls.text: Mixing the two contours breaks not only the architecture but also trust in model metrics.

runtimeBoundary.text: Runtime and delivery are split among Infra, AppOrchestration, AppOrchestration.Interop, Reports, Reports.Runner, and Api. These projects are no longer responsible for the meaning of the model pipeline itself but for startup, layer orchestration, result packaging, and the external surface.

linkedSource.text: It is important to remember that the AppOrchestration project reads a significant part of its source from root AppOrchestration/** through compile-linked files. The project folder alone cannot explain the full orchestration flow.

reportsApiSplit.text: Reports builds and stores documents, while Api reads and serves them outward. If an endpoint starts recalculating what the report layer should have built, the backend loses its clear boundary between build and delivery.

whereToDebug.text: If startup or the orchestration path breaks, go to the root shell, AppOrchestration, and interop. If the document shape diverges, go to Reports. If the problem is only in the HTTP surface and parameters, go to Api. This split saves a lot of localization time.

why-runtimeBoundary.text: These projects sit on the boundary between internal computation and the external behavior of the system.

why-linkedSource.text: Compile-linked source is the main reason why the folder layout here cannot be read literally.

why-reportsApiSplit.text: A clear build-vs-delivery split prevents the HTTP layer from silently replacing upstream computation.

why-whereToDebug.text: Runtime and delivery layers have many similar names, so the correct starting point matters especially here.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Infra holds the shared infrastructure for data access and supporting adapters in the backend.

separation.text: Infra is separated from the domain layers so that filesystem, storage, and low-level access do not replace domain semantics.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when it is already clear that the problem is not in a domain formula but in reading, writing, or technical data access.

dependencies.text: Infra is used by the root flow, train, reports, and part of diagnostics. A defect here often looks domain-level even though the cause is technical.

pitfalls.text: Do not move business rules here just because they need filesystem or cache access.

why-what.text: Shared infra is needed so low-level data access stays centralized rather than being smeared across domain layers.

why-separation.text: This prevents technical plumbing from capturing domain logic.

why-whenOpen.text: If the cause is already localized to IO or storage paths, domain assemblies only add noise.

why-dependencies.text: The wide reuse of infra makes it a frequent source of system-wide symptoms.

why-pitfalls.text: A business rule inside infra quickly breaks the dependency ladder and drags the domain layer downward.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.AppOrchestration is the library orchestration layer of the full pipeline. It assembles the runtime branch, bootstrap, and backtest orchestration separately from the root executable shell.

separation.text: The project is separated from the root console project because orchestration logic should live in a library rather than dissolve into the executable shell.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when you need to understand the orchestration route of the full pipeline, the bootstrap handoff, the backtest data provider, or the compile-linked AppOrchestration/** source.

dependencies.text: The project depends on infra, interop, and domain layers and is then loaded by the root host as the orchestration assembly. It is the library engine that the root shell launches from the outside.

keyFiles.text: Inside the project folder the key local files are BacktestDataProvider.cs and Program.OrchestrationGlobals.cs, but a significant part of the source is linked in from the root AppOrchestration/** folder.

pitfalls.text: If you read only the project folder, orchestration can look almost empty. In reality the main code lives in root source folders and is pulled into this project through compile-linked source.

why-what.text: This is the library orchestration layer that keeps the full pipeline separate from the executable shell.

why-separation.text: This keeps the process entry point and orchestration code from collapsing into one unreadable root project.

why-whenOpen.text: When the problem belongs to the orchestration route rather than Main itself, the root partial files are no longer enough.

why-dependencies.text: Through this project downstream orchestration connects to upstream domain layers without losing the assembly boundary.

why-keyFiles.text: The key local files explain how the project connects to the root linked source and why the project folder is incomplete on its own.

why-pitfalls.text: The main trap is mistaking folder layout for the compile graph and missing the linked source.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.AppOrchestration.Interop connects the root shell, orchestration, and downstream domain calculations through separate interop contracts.

separation.text: Interop is separated so that the link between the executable shell and orchestration does not turn into a dense direct dependency on the full downstream graph.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the root shell and orchestration are correct separately, but the bridge between them breaks: record builders, the omniscient handoff, or interop runtime contracts.

dependencies.text: Interop sits between the root flow and the orchestration/domain layers, so failures here often look like errors “somewhere between layers.”

pitfalls.text: Do not move train or causal domain rules here just for call convenience. Interop should connect layers rather than own their content.

why-what.text: A separate interop layer is needed so different contours can cooperate without merging directly.

why-separation.text: Without interop the boundary between the shell, orchestration, and domain layers quickly blurs.

why-whenOpen.text: If the upstream and downstream look correct separately but the route between them does not, that is a typical interop signal.

why-dependencies.text: Bridge projects almost always produce symptoms on the boundary of several layers at once.

why-pitfalls.text: When interop starts owning domain logic, the whole reason for its existence is lost.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Reports builds and archives backtest, current prediction, and model-level documents. It is the main layer where domain results are translated into persisted report documents.

separation.text: Reports is separated from both the domain layers and Api because document building, document shape, and persistence must be independent from the HTTP surface.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the upstream calculation is already correct but the final report document, archive, or report summary diverges from expectations.

dependencies.text: Reports consumes output from train, omniscient, and the root prediction flow and then feeds storage, Reports.Runner, and Api. It is the transition layer between computation and delivery.

keyFiles.text: The key files in this layer are CurrentPredictionReportBuilder, BacktestReportsOrchestrator, and BacktestSummaryReportBuilder. This is where you can see how domain results are laid out into document blocks and archives.

pitfalls.text: A common mistake is to look here for the cause of a wrong metric even though the builder only packages an already calculated result. If the number arrives wrong, the problem may have been created higher in the stack.

why-what.text: A separate report layer is needed so the shape of persisted documents stays controlled and independent from the delivery mechanism.

why-separation.text: This keeps builder logic from merging with either domain calculations or HTTP endpoints.

why-whenOpen.text: If the upstream data is already verified, the report layer explains how it turned into a concrete document.

why-dependencies.text: Reports connects computation, storage, and delivery, so it is an important boundary between the internal result and the external document.

why-keyFiles.text: These builders show the real packaging of results into documents rather than just a file catalog.

why-pitfalls.text: If the builder is treated as the source of a number, you can spend a long time fixing the consequence instead of the cause.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Reports.Runner is a dedicated CLI contour for listing and regenerating the report archive.

separation.text: Runner is separated from the main report project because operational archive work has its own startup scenario and should not live inside the normal builder flow.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when archive rebuild, the list or replay scenario of report storage, or a dedicated operational run of the reporting contour is broken.

dependencies.text: Runner uses report contracts and report storage, but it is not the main place where domain metrics are built. It is a shell around the report archive lifecycle.

keyFiles.text: The main anchor here is Program.cs, where the CLI flow for archive listing and regeneration is visible.

pitfalls.text: Do not confuse Runner with the main report-builder layer: it works on top of existing report contracts and storage mechanisms.

why-what.text: A separate runner is useful for operational scenarios without starting the full pipeline.

why-separation.text: This keeps the archive lifecycle from cluttering the main builder layer.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Api is the HTTP surface of the backend. The project validates parameters, reads already persisted artifacts, and serves them to the frontend through endpoints and DTOs.

separation.text: Api is separated from Reports because HTTP delivery and report build are different responsibilities. An endpoint should read and validate rather than recalculate domain results on the fly.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when an endpoint contract, parameter validation, DTO mapping, static web-host plumbing, or the route serving current prediction, backtest, or model stats breaks.

dependencies.text: Api depends on report contracts, storage, and read-only services. The frontend consumes this layer downstream, but the truthfulness of the data is determined upstream.

keyFiles.text: The key files are AggregationEndpoints, BacktestEndpoints, CurrentPredictionEndpoints, ModelStatsEndpoints, PfiEndpoints, and RealForecastJournalEndpoints. They show which artifact actually leaves the system and through which contract.

pitfalls.text: If the API returns a wrong number, the cause is not necessarily in the endpoint. Very often the endpoint honestly serves an already corrupted upstream artifact.

why-what.text: A dedicated API layer is needed so the delivery surface is fail-fast and does not replace upstream computation.

why-separation.text: This lets the builder layer and the HTTP layer change independently while preserving a common contract boundary.

why-whenOpen.text: If the upstream artifacts are already built, the endpoint layer is the natural place to check validation and delivery.

why-dependencies.text: Api is the outermost backend layer, so it is sensitive to contract errors upstream, but it must not fix them silently.

why-keyFiles.text: The endpoint files show the external map of the backend from the client’s point of view.

why-pitfalls.text: When an endpoint is taken as the source of a number, the investigation gets stuck on the delivery surface instead of the root layer.
