guardBoundary.text: The backend guard contour is split between Tests, SanityChecks, ArchTests, Analyzers, and their test projects. These are not duplicates of the same idea but different firing levels: runtime behavior, preflight sanity, structural barriers, and static code guards.

testsVsAnalyzers.text: Regular tests verify executable behavior and contracts during execution. Analyzers and architecture barriers work earlier and forbid dangerous code or dependency shapes before runtime.

archRules.text: A separate ArchTests project and analyzer packages matter because some defects should not be waited for until a report diverges numerically. If the dependency direction is already broken, the project should fail at the structural level.

failureStrategy.text: This part of the backend shows the overall fail-fast strategy clearly: instead of silent fallbacks the project tries to fail where the cause is still directly readable. That is why the guard layers are split so early and so finely.

why-guardBoundary.text: Each of these layers catches its own defect class at its own lifecycle stage.

why-testsVsAnalyzers.text: The earlier a violation can be proven, the cheaper it is to fix and the less downstream noise it creates.

why-archRules.text: Structural problems are easier to catch where they have not yet changed into runtime symptoms.

why-failureStrategy.text: Fail-fast is expressed architecturally here, not only in error messages.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Tests is the main test suite of the backend. It gathers checks for domain layers, orchestration, the delivery surface, and shared invariants of time, leakage, and documents.

separation.text: The main test project is separated from ArchTests and analyzer packages because runtime tests, architecture barriers, and static analysis solve different defect classes and should fail at different stages.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when you need to know whether a regression check already exists for a concrete domain scenario, leakage boundary, report contract, bootstrap route, or API contract.

dependencies.text: This project pulls almost the whole backend graph as the subject under test. It is a good way to see which nodes are considered critical for system stability.

pitfalls.text: Do not confuse Tests with the place where reference-level architecture barriers live or with analyzer rules for the IDE. The main suite verifies behavior and contracts during test execution.

why-what.text: The main test suite shows which scenarios the project considers important enough to reproduce in code.

why-separation.text: Behavioral tests, architecture barriers, and analyzers produce different kinds of signals and must not collapse into one layer.

why-whenOpen.text: Before adding a new guard it is useful to know whether a neighboring suite already exists at the right abstraction level.

why-dependencies.text: The wide dependency coverage makes this project a map of real systemic risks.

why-pitfalls.text: If you try to solve an architectural problem only with a runtime test, the defect will be caught too late.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.SanityChecks is a dedicated operational self-check layer for checks such as NY windowing and other sanity scenarios.

separation.text: The project is separated from the main test suite because sanity checks are needed as a fast operational preflight and do not always match the shape of unit or integration tests.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when a quick signal is needed that basic expectations about time and data are already broken before the full pipeline runs.

dependencies.text: SanityChecks sits closer to operational execution than ordinary test suites and uses the key backend contracts as guard rails.

pitfalls.text: Do not replace full regression tests with sanity checks. Their role is to stop an obviously bad run quickly rather than describe behavior exhaustively.

why-what.text: An operational sanity layer is needed as an early fuse before a heavy pipeline run.

why-separation.text: This keeps quick preflight checks from mixing with the longer testing contour.

why-whenOpen.text: If what you need is a quick signal before a full run, the regular suite may already be too heavy.

why-dependencies.text: This layer shows which basic invariants the project treats as mandatory even before normal runtime.

why-pitfalls.text: Sanity checks should complement test coverage rather than replace it.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.ArchTests is a dedicated project for architecture barriers. It checks forbidden references, anti-direction rules, and other structural guards at the test level.

separation.text: ArchTests is separated from the main test suite so structural rules stay visible as a distinct layer rather than drowning among domain scenarios.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when dependency direction, a layer boundary, or a forbidden-reference rule between assemblies changes.

dependencies.text: The project depends on the assembly map and structural rules rather than on concrete numerical scenarios. Its failures signal a broken architectural shape rather than a wrong metric.

keyFiles.text: The key anchors here are ArchBarrierTests, AntiDirectionComparisonPrinterTests, and Baseline1mWindowAllowGapTests.

pitfalls.text: Do not move ordinary behavioral regression scenarios here. This project exists specifically for dependency shape and structural rules.

why-what.text: Architecture tests turn the desired layer scheme into a machine-checkable rule.

why-separation.text: This keeps the structural signal from getting lost among behavioral tests.

why-whenOpen.text: If the direction rule of references changes, the behavioral suite is no longer sufficient.

why-dependencies.text: ArchTests is sensitive to the shape of the solution as a whole rather than to one implementation.

why-keyFiles.text: These files show which structural restrictions are truly considered mandatory.

why-pitfalls.text: When a structural rule is checked only indirectly through runtime, the defect is caught too late.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Analyzers is the package of Roslyn and static-analyzer rules for backend code. It catches dangerous patterns in the IDE and during build.

separation.text: Analyzers are separated from test projects because static code shape is better caught before code execution and before the test contour runs.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when a static prohibition rule changes: dependency, DateTime usage, OrderBy anti-pattern, namespace guard, or provenance contract.

dependencies.text: The analyzer package looks at code as text and syntax rather than as an executable scenario. It is the earliest guard among all quality layers.

keyFiles.text: The key rules here are CausalAssemblyDependencyAnalyzer, CausalDateTimeAnalyzer, CausalOrderByAnalyzer, CausalNamespaceGuards, and CausalProvenanceContractAnalyzer.

pitfalls.text: Do not turn an analyzer into a repetition of runtime tests. It exists for recurring syntactic and structural anti-patterns that are worth catching before code execution.

why-what.text: A static analyzer provides the earliest feedback for recurring violations of code conventions.

why-separation.text: This separates syntactic and structural guards from the behavioral signal of tests.

why-whenOpen.text: If a rule can be checked from code before execution, an analyzer is almost always better than a test as the first line of defense.

why-dependencies.text: An analyzer depends on code shape rather than the runtime graph, so it complements the test layers rather than replacing them.

why-keyFiles.text: These files show which anti-patterns the project considers critical for the quality of causal code.

why-pitfalls.text: An analyzer should not try to compute business truth; its task is to catch forbidden code shapes.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Analyzers.Tests tests the analyzer rules and their diagnostics themselves.

separation.text: Analyzer tests are separated from the general test suite because they do not verify backend domain behavior but the correctness of the static rules themselves.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when a diagnostic id, message, code-fix expectation, or coverage for an analyzer rule changes.

dependencies.text: The project depends on the analyzer package and Roslyn test harnesses. Its responsibilities are narrow but critical for trust in static guards.

keyFiles.text: The current main anchor is CausalAssemblyDependencyAnalyzerTests.

pitfalls.text: Do not write such checks in the main backend test suite: they belong to the analyzer ecosystem rather than to product runtime.

why-what.text: Analyzer tests are needed so that the static guard itself does not become a source of false signals.

why-separation.text: This keeps analyzer-rule verification from mixing with domain regression scenarios.

why-whenOpen.text: When the diagnostic itself changes, neither ArchTests nor the regular suite can verify it correctly.

why-dependencies.text: This layer makes trust in the analyzer package verifiable rather than declarative.

why-keyFiles.text: A single test file matters more than a deep tree here because it shows the reference way to verify analyzer diagnostics.

why-pitfalls.text: If an analyzer is not tested separately, the static guard begins to drift silently.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.CachePath.Analyzers is a dedicated analyzer package for conventions around cache-path construction.

separation.text: The project is separated from the general analyzer package because its rules belong to a narrow infrastructure convention and should not be lost among causal guards.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the rule against direct cache-path construction or the diagnostic around the cache-path API changes.

dependencies.text: The package inspects the code shape of an infrastructure convention and does not depend on any concrete runtime scenario.

keyFiles.text: The main anchor is DirectCachePathConstructionAnalyzer and its related diagnostics.

pitfalls.text: Do not add broad causal rules here. This package exists specifically for cache-path conventions and their fail-fast enforcement.

why-what.text: Narrow infrastructure conventions are better kept in a separate analyzer package so the area of responsibility stays sharp.

why-separation.text: This prevents cache-path guards from being mixed with unrelated static rules.

why-whenOpen.text: If the cache-path contract itself changes, searching the general analyzer package is slower and noisier.

why-dependencies.text: The rules in this package are small, but they protect against a recurring infrastructure anti-pattern.

why-keyFiles.text: The key analyzer file shows the entry point to the whole convention.

why-pitfalls.text: If such a package becomes too broad, it turns unreadable and useless.

what.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.CachePath.Analyzers.Tests tests the diagnostics of the cache-path analyzer package.

separation.text: These tests are separated because they verify the quality of the static guard itself rather than backend runtime behavior.

whenOpen.text: Open this project when the diagnostic text, coverage rule, or expected violation handling for cache paths changes.

dependencies.text: The project depends only on the analyzer package and the test harnesses, so it remains very narrow and focused.

pitfalls.text: Do not move these checks into the general backend suite: there they lose locality and clarity of cause.

why-what.text: Separate tests are needed so the cache-path analyzer does not drift without control.

why-separation.text: This keeps the narrow infrastructure diagnostic from getting lost among ordinary regression tests.

why-whenOpen.text: When the analyzer rule itself changes, the runtime suite says nothing about the correctness of the diagnostics.

why-dependencies.text: A small test layer makes the quality of the static guard measurable.

why-keyFiles.text: A single test file here shows the full expected contract of the analyzer rule.

why-pitfalls.text: If such tests dissolve into the general suite, the cause of a failure becomes much less obvious.

rootSourceFolders.text: The project root contains not only the solution and project folders but also working source directories. The most important ones here are AppOrchestration/** and Diagnostics/**: they physically live at the root yet participate in the compile graph and cannot be treated as random neighboring folders.

excludedEmpty.text: CurrentPrediction and Utils are not expanded as working source nodes in the current snapshot because they contain no active code of the main backend graph. It is important to show them specifically as exclusions so that a similar name does not send the investigation into an empty directory.

excludedGenerated.text: Generated and service directories such as bin, obj, artifacts, cache, .tmp-run, logs, and IDE folders are intentionally excluded from the tree. They may matter for environment debugging, but they are not working source nodes of the backend architecture.

inactiveSnapshot.text: SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Core.Causal.Trusted is separately marked as an inactive snapshot folder: its strong name makes it a very tempting false entry point even though it has no active role in the current main solution.

why-rootSourceFolders.text: Root source folders belong on the map because the project compile graph does not match the directory layout here.

why-excludedEmpty.text: Empty directories with strong names create the most expensive false search paths.

why-excludedGenerated.text: An architectural map must show working source rather than side artifacts of build and execution.

why-inactiveSnapshot.text: Inactive snapshot nodes are especially dangerous where their names sound like key domain layers.

what.text: The root AppOrchestration folder contains a significant part of the compile-linked source for SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.AppOrchestration.csproj. It is not just a neighboring directory but an active working source contour of the orchestration layer.

separation.text: The folder lives outside the project directory because the orchestration source is historically and structurally stored at the root while being compiled into a separate assembly. This matters for understanding the project as much as the project references themselves.

whenOpen.text: Open this folder when the AppOrchestration project folder makes it look like there is almost no code there, or when you need to understand the real orchestration flow through compile-linked source.

dependencies.text: This folder physically sits at the root but logically belongs to the AppOrchestration project. Errors here show up as ordinary orchestration defects even though the layout does not make them obvious.

pitfalls.text: The most common mistake is to treat AppOrchestration/** as a separate project or, conversely, as an inactive historical tail. In reality it is active source for the current orchestration layer.

why-what.text: This node belongs on the map precisely because compile-linked source is invisible if you look only at project folders.

why-separation.text: The root layout and the assembly boundary do not match here, and this must be explained explicitly.

why-whenOpen.text: When the project folder looks empty, the root source folders become the only way to read the real orchestration code.

why-dependencies.text: A file’s physical location does not change which assembly it belongs to at compile time.

why-pitfalls.text: Without this note the project layout inevitably misleads even an experienced developer.

what.text: The root Diagnostics folder contains active source code for diagnostic routes outside ordinary project folders.

separation.text: The folder is shown separately because it physically lives at the root and, from layout alone, easily looks accidental even though it participates in the working source contour.

whenOpen.text: Open this folder when diagnostics are already localized to the root diagnostic source rather than to a report builder or a test suite.

dependencies.text: As with AppOrchestration/**, the physical location of the folder does not mean it is outside the compile graph. It is active source connected to working code.

pitfalls.text: If root Diagnostics is treated as a mere auxiliary dump folder, the real cause of a diagnostic route can be missed.

why-what.text: Root source folders matter exactly where the compile graph does not match the folder layout.

why-separation.text: This lets the map show active source that would otherwise look like a random root folder.

why-whenOpen.text: When diagnostic logic really lives in root source, searching project folders only wastes time.

why-dependencies.text: The compile graph is defined by whether a file is included in a project rather than by its location on disk.

why-pitfalls.text: The root-folder name alone is too weak a signal to reveal its real role without a hint like this.

header.title: Runtime flow from startup to artifacts
header.subtitle: This page breaks down the path from the entry point to bootstrap, current prediction, backtest, and artifact persistence.

consoleProgram.text: The main console execution starts in `Program.cs` and the partial orchestration files.

apiProgram.text: The HTTP contour is started separately through `SolSignalModel1D_Backtest.Api/Program.cs`.

why-runtime-console-program.text: This is where the order of bootstrap, train/infer, diagnostics, and report flow is visible.

why-runtime-api-program.text: The API must not silently retrain the model or rebuild reports on user request.

role.text: This node owns the top orchestration route in `Program.cs` / `HostMain.cs` / `Prediction.cs`.

why-runtime-entry-console-role.text: If a change touches the top orchestration route in `Program.cs` / `HostMain.cs` / `Prediction.cs`, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the HTTP host and endpoint publication through `Api/Program.cs`.

why-runtime-entry-api-role.text: If a change touches the HTTP host and endpoint publication through `Api/Program.cs`, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the standalone runner for report rebuild.

why-runtime-entry-reports-role.text: If a change touches the standalone runner for report rebuild, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

bootstrapCandles.text: Bootstrap first loads and validates candles and only then builds derived sets.

bootstrapDailyRows.text: Indicators and daily rows are then built for train/infer and backtest stages.

why-runtime-bootstrap-candles.text: If candle coverage or ordering is broken, the rest of the pipeline is already unreliable.

why-runtime-bootstrap-dailyrows.text: Daily rows bridge raw market data with domain ML input.

role.text: This node owns loading of candles and timeframes in the bootstrap stage.

why-runtime-bootstrap-loadcandles-role.text: If a change touches loading of candles and timeframes in the bootstrap stage, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns construction of indicators and causal daily rows.

why-runtime-bootstrap-indicators-role.text: If a change touches construction of indicators and causal daily rows, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the infrastructure and data layer of the bootstrap stage.

why-runtime-bootstrap-infra-role.text: If a change touches the infrastructure and data layer of the bootstrap stage, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

predictionPipeline.text: Current prediction uses causal rows, train artifacts, and scope-specific engines for the live forecast of the next trading day.

causalBoundary.text: The critical boundary runs between causal preparation and the parts that may rely on offline knowledge without seeing the day outcome.

why-runtime-prediction-pipeline.text: The live forecast is assembled by the offline pipeline layer, while the API only reads the result.

why-runtime-prediction-boundary.text: This is where the leakage boundary is easiest to blur silently.

role.text: This node owns runtime inference engines for current prediction.

why-runtime-prediction-infer-role.text: If a change touches runtime inference engines for current prediction, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the training boundary and train artifacts used by inference.

why-runtime-prediction-train-role.text: If a change touches the training boundary and train artifacts used by inference, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns interop assembly of the runtime prediction context.

why-runtime-prediction-interop-role.text: If a change touches interop assembly of the runtime prediction context, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

backtestFlow.text: The backtest flow takes bootstrap output plus trained state and builds history records, diagnostics, and report artifacts.

currentPredictionHistory.text: Current-prediction history is backfilled from historical records through the shared report flow.

why-runtime-backtest-flow.text: Historical evaluation and the live forecast use shared domain layers, but they are assembled by different orchestration branches.

why-runtime-backtest-history.text: The UI shows an archive built from backtest output rather than a separate manual journal.

role.text: This node owns orchestration of backtest, diagnostics, and report persistence.

why-runtime-backtest-orchestration-role.text: If a change touches orchestration of backtest, diagnostics, and report persistence, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns assembly of backtest summary, diagnostics, PFI, and aggregation artifacts.

why-runtime-backtest-reports-role.text: If a change touches assembly of backtest summary, diagnostics, PFI, and aggregation artifacts, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns backfilled current-prediction history from historical records.

why-runtime-backtest-current-role.text: If a change touches backfilled current-prediction history from historical records, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

header.title: Reports, storage, and API surface
header.subtitle: This page explains where the backend builds document contracts, how it stores them by kind and id, and through which endpoints they reach the frontend.

reportBuilders.text: Builders assemble backend artifacts into the canonical document contract with section/item/table keys.

documentContract.text: For the frontend, the stable shape of the persisted document matters more than the internal train-pipeline implementation.

why-delivery-report-builders.text: Any builder change can become a contract change for the API and UI.

why-delivery-document-contract.text: A broken document contract easily slips past the compiler, so it must be documented explicitly.

role.text: This node owns the current-prediction document builder and policy execution table.

why-delivery-current-builder-role.text: If a change touches the current-prediction document builder and policy execution table, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the backtest summary document builder.

why-delivery-summary-builder-role.text: If a change touches the backtest summary document builder, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the full orchestration layer of the report flow.

why-delivery-orchestrator-role.text: If a change touches the full orchestration layer of the report flow, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

reportStorage.text: `ReportStorage` persists documents by kind and id and can resolve latest, daily history, and scope-specific current-prediction sets.

realForecastJournalStorage.text: The real forecast journal is stored separately because it is an immutable operational journal rather than a normal latest report.

why-delivery-report-storage.text: An endpoint usually recalculates nothing and only reads storage.

why-delivery-rfj-storage.text: The journal preserves the session-open snapshot and day finalization as separate artifacts.

role.text: This node owns kind/id storage, latest lookup, and report history indexing.

why-delivery-report-storage-role.text: If a change touches kind/id storage, latest lookup, and report history indexing, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns immutable storage for the real forecast and its archive.

why-delivery-rfj-storage-role.text: If a change touches immutable storage for the real forecast and its archive, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

apiEndpoints.text: The API layer validates parameters and returns fail-fast errors if the required artifact is missing or its contract is outdated.

workerFlow.text: A separate worker and service contour maintains the immutable real-forecast journal.

why-delivery-api-endpoints.text: The API serves a read-only surface over already-built artifacts.

why-delivery-worker-flow.text: The journal needs capture and finalize logic because it stores the forecast at decision time separately from the day outcome.

role.text: This node owns the live/dates/by-date API for current prediction.

why-delivery-api-current-role.text: If a change touches the live/dates/by-date API for current prediction, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the HTTP surface for backtest, aggregation, and PFI.

why-delivery-api-backtest-role.text: If a change touches the HTTP surface for backtest, aggregation, and PFI, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the operational API around the immutable real-forecast journal.

why-delivery-api-rfj-role.text: If a change touches the operational API around the immutable real-forecast journal, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

frontendConsumes.text: The frontend consumes the backend as a set of already-persisted documents and endpoint responses.

noUiRecalc.text: An empty response or a 404 usually means the required artifact is missing or the storage or endpoint contract is broken.

why-delivery-frontend-consumes.text: Artifact generation and storage remain the backend responsibility rather than the UI’s responsibility.

why-delivery-no-ui-recalc.text: A silent frontend fallback hides the cause and makes debugging harder.

header.title: Tests, analyzers, and trust guards
header.subtitle: This page gathers the protection layers that keep time, causal, and report contracts from silently degrading.

coverageShape.text: The main test project covers domain layers, orchestration, and the delivery surface.

leakageDepth.text: The critical suites are the ones holding the time, split, and leakage boundary.

why-guards-coverage-shape.text: The test structure reveals the project risk map and where a new check belongs.

why-guards-leakage-depth.text: Time and causality correctness matter more to the backend than cosmetic report details.

role.text: This node owns the main catalog of backend test suites.

why-guards-tests-suites-role.text: If a change touches the main catalog of backend test suites, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the sensitive leakage, current-prediction, and backtest scenarios.

why-guards-tests-leakage-role.text: If a change touches the sensitive leakage, current-prediction, and backtest scenarios, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

archTests.text: Architecture tests forbid dangerous references and layer violations before runtime.

layerBarriers.text: For the causal versus omniscient boundary, one extra dependency can blur the whole trust boundary.

why-guards-archtests.text: Architecture rules turn the desired layer scheme into a machine-checkable rule.

why-guards-layer-barriers.text: This guards the cause rather than the symptoms of downstream errors.

role.text: This node owns the dedicated project for architecture rules.

why-guards-arch-project-role.text: If a change touches the dedicated project for architecture rules, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns architecture checks inside the main test suite.

why-guards-arch-main-suite-role.text: If a change touches architecture checks inside the main test suite, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

causalAnalyzers.text: Code analyzers provide feedback before tests even run.

cachePathAnalyzers.text: Dedicated analyzer packages enforce infrastructure conventions as early as architecture tests enforce assembly boundaries.

why-guards-causal-analyzers.text: Analyzers are useful for recurring anti-patterns that should be caught in the IDE and CI.

why-guards-cachepath-analyzers.text: Static checks prevent conventions such as cache paths from being broken silently.

role.text: This node owns Roslyn and static analyzers for backend code.

why-guards-analyzers-project-role.text: If a change touches Roslyn and static analyzers for backend code, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns tests for the analyzer rules themselves.

why-guards-analyzers-tests-role.text: If a change touches tests for the analyzer rules themselves, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

selfCheckRunner.text: The self-check can be attached to pipeline startup through `SelfCheckRunner.RunAsync`, but it is not enabled by default for every run.

shuffleSanity.text: SanityChecks verify that operational expectations do not contradict the basic trust boundary.

why-guards-selfcheck-runner.text: The self-check can stop the pipeline before report generation if the basic validation set fails.

why-guards-shuffle-sanity.text: This is a trust fuse for data and time rather than a way to silently survive an error.

role.text: This node owns the operational self-check project.

why-guards-sanity-project-role.text: If a change touches the operational self-check project, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.

role.text: This node owns the `SelfCheckRunner.RunAsync` hook inside Program.cs.

why-guards-program-hook-role.text: If a change touches the `SelfCheckRunner.RunAsync` hook inside Program.cs, inspect this node and its immediate dependencies first.
